# Helicity at Small $x$: Oscillations Generated by Bringing Back the Quarks

> We construct a numerical solution of the recently-derived large-$N_c \& N_f$ small-$x$ helicity evolution equations with the aim to establish the small-$x$ asymptotics of the quark helicity distribution beyond the large-$N_c$ limit explored previously in the same framework. (Here $N_c$ and $N_f$ are the numbers of quark colors and flavors.) While the large-$N_c$ helicity evolution involves gluons only, the large-$N_c \& N_f$ evolution includes contributions from quarks as well. We find that adding quarks to the evolution makes quark helicity distribution oscillate as a function of $x$. Our numerical results in the large-$N_c \& N_f$ limit lead to the $x$-dependence of the flavor-singlet quark helicity distribution which is well-approximated by \begin{align} ΔΣ(x, Q^2)\bigg|_{\mbox{large-}N_c \& N_f} \sim \left( \frac{1}{x} \right)^{α_h^q} \, \cos \left[ ω_q \, \ln \left( \frac{1}{x} \right) + φ_q \right]. \end{align} The power $α_h^q$ exhibits a weak $N_f$-dependence, and, for all $N_f$ values considered, remains very close to $α_h^q (N_f=0) = (4/\sqrt{3}) \sqrt{α_s N_c/(2 π)}$ obtained earlier in the large-$N_c$ limit. The novel oscillation frequency $ω_q$ and phase shift $φ_q$ depend more strongly on the number of flavors $N_f$ (with $ω_q =0$ in the pure-glue large-$N_c$ limit). The typical period of oscillations for $ΔΣ$ is rather long, spanning many units of rapidity. We speculate whether the oscillations we find are related to the sign variation with $x$ seen in the strange quark helicity distribution extracted from the data.

## Key claim

Adding quarks to the large-\(N_c\) small-\(x\) helicity evolution (moving to the large-\(N_c \& N_f\) limit) causes the flavor-singlet quark helicity distribution \(\Delta\Sigma(x,Q^2)\) to oscillate as a function of \(\ln(1/x)\), whereas the pure-gluon large-\(N_c\) limit previously yielded only a power-law growth with no oscillations.

## Method

The authors numerically solved the recently-derived large-\(N_c \& N_f\) small-\(x\) helicity evolution equations in the double logarithmic approximation (DLA), which mix polarized quark dipole amplitudes \(Q\) and polarized gluon dipole amplitudes \(G\) along with auxiliary "neighbor" dipole amplitudes \(\Gamma\) and \(\overline{\Gamma}\). The integral equations were discretized and recast into a recursive form to enable efficient numerical evaluation over a grid in the transformed variables \(\eta\) (rapidity) and \(s\) (dipole size), using initial conditions \(G^{(0)} = Q^{(0)} = 1\) and running the evolution for \(N_f = 2, 3, 6\).

## Key numbers

* The small-\(x\) asymptotics of the flavor-singlet quark helicity distribution is given by \(\Delta\Sigma(x, Q^2) \sim (1/x)^{\alpha_h^q} \cos[\omega_q \ln(1/x) + \varphi_q]\).
* The power \(\alpha_h^q\) remains very close to the large-\(N_c\) value \(\alpha_h^q(N_f=0) = (4/\sqrt{3}) \sqrt{\alpha_s N_c/(2\pi)}\) for all \(N_f\) values considered.
* The oscillation frequency \(\omega_q\) is zero in the pure-glue large-\(N_c\) limit and increases with \(N_f\), meaning the oscillation period shortens as more quark flavors become dynamically relevant.
* The typical period of oscillations for \(\Delta\Sigma\) spans many units of rapidity.

## Context

This work extends the earlier large-\(N_c\) helicity evolution calculations by Kovchegov, Pitonyak, and Sievert (2016-2017), which found only power-law growth \(\Delta\Sigma \sim (1/x)^{\alpha_h^q}\) without oscillations. The new oscillatory behavior arises from including quark contributions in the large-\(N_c \& N_f\) limit, which were absent in the pure-gluon large-\(N_c\) framework. The results enable extrapolation of \(\Delta\Sigma\) to smaller \(x\) values to estimate the total quark contribution to the proton spin \(S_q\), and the authors speculate whether these oscillations relate to the sign variation with \(x\) seen in strange quark helicity distributions extracted from experimental data by de Florian et al. (2009), Ball et al. (2013), and Ethier et al. (2017).
